data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-24T01:30:52.482Z</current_datetime>

I have this data-driven test using object arrays. Is there a better way?

```csharp
[TestMethod]
[DynamicData(nameof(TestData))]
public void Validate_ReturnsExpected(string input, bool expected)
{
    var result = Validator.IsValid(input);
    Assert.AreEqual(expected, result);
}

public static IEnumerable<object[]> TestData =>
[
    ["valid@email.com", true],
    ["invalid", false],
    ["", false],
];
```

data.content:
Yes — use **`[DataRow]`** for simple inline cases, which gives you type safety and avoids the `object[]` ceremony:

```csharp
[TestMethod]
[DataRow("valid@email.com", true)]
[DataRow("invalid", false)]
[DataRow("", false)]
public void Validate_ReturnsExpected(string input, bool expected)
{
    var result = Validator.IsValid(input);
    Assert.AreEqual(expected, result);
}
```

**When to use which:**

| Approach | Use when |
|---|---|
| `[DataRow]` | Compile-time constants (strings, bools, ints) |
| `[DynamicData]` | Complex objects, computed data, or non-constant values |

If you do need `DynamicData` for complex cases, you can improve type safety with `DynamicData<T>` (MSTest 3.7+):

```csharp
public static IEnumerable<(string Input, bool Expected)> TestData =>
[
    ("valid@email.com", true),
    ("invalid", false),
];
```

`DataRow` is the better fit for your current scenario — it's simpler, type-checked at compile time, and each case shows up as a distinct test in the runner.
data.reasoningText: The user is asking about improving their MSTest data-driven test that uses `DynamicData` with `object[]`. The main downside of `object[]` is the lack of type safety. MSTest has better alternatives.
